html:
[1407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 異史氏曰 - Formal expression used by the author to introduce his commentary or opinion.
  • 噫嘻 - Interjection expressing surprise or wonder.
  • 造物之殊不由人也 - Means 'the extraordinary creations of nature are not subject to human will.'
  • 益仇之而益福之 - Means 'the more they hate him, the more they bless him.'
  • 彼机诈者无谓甚矣 - Means 'those who rely on cunning and deceit are truly insignificant.'
  • 顾受其爱敬；而反以得祸 - Means 'but they receive his love and respect, yet they end up suffering misfortune.'
  • 不更奇哉 - Means 'isn't this even more extraordinary?'
  • 盗泉之水，一掬亦污也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'even a sip from the Spring of Thieves is still tainted,' referring to the corrupting nature of evil.
  • 尽掘七十二疑冢，必有一冢葬君尸 - Famous line from poetry, meaning 'if all seventy-two tombs are dug up, one must contain the emperor's body.'
  • 宁知竟在七十二冢之外乎 - Means 'who would have thought it would be outside the seventy-two tombs?'
  • 奸哉瞒也 - Means 'how deceitful is Cao Cao!'
  • 千余年而朽骨不保 - Means 'after more than a thousand years, his bones are not even preserved.'
  • 变诈亦复何益 - Means 'what good does deceit bring?'
  • 呜呼，瞒之智正瞒之愚也 - Means 'alas, Cao Cao's wisdom is truly his folly.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 異史氏 - Refers to the author of the *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio* (聊斋志异), Pu Songling.
  • 曹操 - Refers to Cao Cao (155–220), a prominent warlord and statesman of the late Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 七十二疑冢 - Refers to the seventy-two tombs built by Cao Cao to confuse his burial place, as described in historical and literary sources.
  • 盗泉 - A metaphor for evil or corruption, derived from the story of a spring that was avoided by the virtuous but tainted by the wicked.

Proper Nouns

  • 異史氏 - The author Pu Songling.
  • 曹操 - The historical figure Cao Cao.
  • 七十二疑冢 - The seventy-two tombs built by Cao Cao.
  • 盗泉 - The metaphorical spring of corruption.

[1408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 少薄行，无检幅 - Young, with poor conduct and lacking in restraint or propriety.
  • 醉后昏眊 - Dazed and confused after drinking alcohol.
  • 竟忘其死 - Completely forgot that the other person had died.
  • 冥间何作 - What do you do in the afterlife?
  • 转轮王殿下司录 - Serving as a recorder in the afterlife under the King of Wheel.
  • 尊名在黑暗狱中 - Your name is recorded in the dark prison.
  • 恶籍盈指 - Record of evil deeds filled to the point of overflowing.
  • 洗心改行 - To cleanse one's heart and change one's behavior.
  • 不敢差跌 - Not to make mistakes or falter.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 安庆戴生 - Refers to a specific individual named Dai Sheng from Anqing.
  • 故表兄季生 - Refers to a specific individual named Ji Sheng, the person's former cousin.
  • 转轮王 - The King of Wheel, a Buddhist deity associated with the afterlife.
  • 黑暗狱 - A dark prison in the afterlife, often associated with punishment for evil deeds in Buddhist and Taoist traditions.

Proper Nouns

  • 安庆戴生 - The name of the person from Anqing.
  • 故表兄季生 - The name of the person's former cousin.
  • 转轮王 - The King of Wheel, a Buddhist deity.
  • 黑暗狱 - The dark prison in the afterlife.

[1409]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先是 - Temporal marker meaning 'before this' or 'previously,' setting the stage for the events that follow.
  • 戴私其邻妇 - Literally 'Dai privately desired his neighbor's wife,' where '私' means 'to desire' or 'to have a secret affection for.'
  • 思掩执之 - Literally 'thought to secretly catch her,' where '掩执' means 'to secretly seize' or 'to ambush and capture.'
  • 而戴自改行 - Literally 'but Dai himself changed his ways,' where '自改行' means 'changed his behavior' or 'reformed himself.'
  • 永与妇绝 - Literally 'forever severed ties with the wife,' where '永' means 'forever' and '绝' means 'to sever' or 'to break off.'
  • 阳与语 - Literally 'pretended to talk to her,' where '阳' means 'pretend' or 'act as if,' and '与语' means 'to converse with.'
  • 给窥眢井 - Literally 'offered to look into a dry well,' where '给' means 'to offer' or 'to pretend,' '窥' means 'to peep' or 'to look into,' and '眢井' means 'a dry well.'
  • 因而堕之 - Literally 'and thus fell into it,' where '因而' means 'thus' or 'therefore,' and '堕之' means 'fell into it.'
  • 中夜苏 - Literally 'awoke at midnight,' where '中夜' means 'midnight' and '苏' means 'to awaken' or 'to revive.'
  • 坐井中大号 - Literally 'sat in the well and cried loudly,' where '坐' means 'to sit,' '井中' means 'in the well,' and '大号' means 'cried loudly.'
  • 殊无知者 - Literally 'nobody knew,' where '殊' means 'nobody' or 'not at all,' and '知者' means 'those who know.'
  • 以为恨 - Literally 'held it as a grudge,' where '以为' means 'to regard as' and '恨' means 'grudge' or 'resentment.'
  • 过宿往听之 - Literally 'went to listen to it the next day,' where '过宿' means 'the next day' or 'after a night,' and '往听之' means 'went to listen to it.'
  • 闻其声 - Literally 'heard his voice,' where '闻' means 'to hear' and '其声' means 'his voice.'
  • 急投石 - Literally 'quickly threw stones,' where '急' means 'quickly' and '投石' means 'to throw stones.'
  • 移避洞中 - Literally 'moved to avoid in a cave,' where '移避' means 'to move to avoid' and '洞中' means 'in a cave.'
  • 不敢复作声 - Literally 'dared not make a sound again,' where '不敢' means 'dared not,' '复' means 'again,' and '作声' means 'to make a sound.'
  • 劚土填井 - Literally 'dug soil to fill the well,' where '劚' means 'to dig' and '填井' means 'to fill the well.'
  • 几满之 - Literally 'almost filled it,' where '几' means 'almost' and '满之' means 'filled it.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 戴私其邻妇 - This phrase involves a moral transgression, reflecting the social norms and taboos of the time regarding adultery.
  • 思掩执之 - The act of secretly trying to catch someone is a common motif in classical literature, often used to illustrate the consequences of one's actions.
  • 而戴自改行 - The concept of personal reform and redemption is a recurring theme in Chinese literature, emphasizing the possibility of moral improvement.
  • 永与妇绝 - The idea of severing ties with a lover or family member is a significant cultural and social issue, often depicted in classical texts.
  • 阳与语 - The use of deception and pretense in social interactions is a common theme in classical literature, highlighting the complexities of human relationships.
  • 给窥眢井 - The dry well is a symbol of danger and entrapment, often used in classical literature to represent a trap or a difficult situation.
  • 因而堕之 - The act of falling into a well is a metaphor for being caught in a dangerous or inescapable situation.
  • 中夜苏 - The concept of awakening at midnight is often associated with moments of realization or crisis in classical literature.
  • 坐井中大号 - The image of sitting in a well and crying loudly is a vivid description of distress and helplessness.
  • 殊无知者 - The idea of being completely ignored or unnoticed is a common theme in classical literature, often used to highlight the isolation of the protagonist.
  • 以为恨 - Holding a grudge is a common human emotion, and the passage reflects the long-lasting nature of such feelings.
  • 过宿往听之 - The act of waiting and listening is a common motif in classical literature, often used to build tension and suspense.
  • 闻其声 - The act of hearing a voice is a significant moment in many classical stories, often leading to important revelations.
  • 急投石 - The act of throwing stones is a violent and aggressive action, reflecting the neighbor's anger and desire for retribution.
  • 移避洞中 - The act of moving to a cave is a survival strategy, often used in classical literature to depict a character's desperate situation.
  • 不敢复作声 - The fear of making a sound again is a vivid description of the character's state of mind, emphasizing his fear and caution.
  • 劚土填井 - The act of digging soil to fill a well is a practical and determined action, reflecting the neighbor's resolve to ensure Dai's death.
  • 几满之 - The near-filling of the well is a dramatic and tense moment, building suspense and highlighting the neighbor's persistence.

Proper Nouns

  • 戴 - A proper noun referring to the main character, Dai.
  • 邻人 - A proper noun referring to the neighbor who harbors resentment against Dai.

[1410]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 洞中冥黑真与地狱无异 - Describes the darkness in the cave as being indistinguishable from hell.
  • 计无生理 - Means there is no hope of survival.
  • 葡匐渐入 - Describes the act of crawling slowly into the cave.
  • 但见诸磷渐浮水来 - Describes the phosphorescent lights gradually floating towards us.
  • 磷火浮游，荧荧满洞 - Describes the phosphorescent lights floating and illuminating the entire cave.
  • 闻青燐悉为冤鬼 - Means hearing that the green phosphorescence is all of wronged ghosts.
  • 如可共话，亦慰寂寞 - Means if we can converse, it will also comfort our loneliness.
  • 思我辈冷水浸骨，超拔无日 - Means thinking that our bones are soaked with cold water, and we have no day to be exalted.
  • 倘再履人世，祈捞残骨葬一义冢 - Means if I return to the human world again, I will pray to retrieve the remaining bones and bury them in a just grave.
  • 深在九地，安望重睹天日乎 - Means being deeply buried in the nine layers of the earth, how can I hope to see the sky again?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 重泉下 - Refers to the underworld or the netherworld.
  • 佛号 - Refers to the recitation of Buddhist mantras or names.
  • 磷火 - Refers to phosphorescent lights, often associated with spirits or ghosts.
  • 冤鬼 - Refers to wronged spirits or ghosts.
  • 城隍 - Refers to the guardian deity of a city or town.
  • 相公 - Refers to a scholar or literatus.
  • 龙飞相公 - Refers to a scholar or literatus who has the power to control water.
  • 决地海之水 - Means cutting off the waters of the earth and sea.
  • 超拔 - Refers to being exalted or liberated from suffering.
  • 义冢 - Refers to a public cemetery for unknown or unclaimed bodies.

Proper Nouns

  • 龙飞相公 - The name of the literatus who controlled the water and caused the flooding.
  • 城隍 - The guardian deity of the city or town.
  • 义冢 - The name of the public cemetery where the narrator plans to bury the ghosts' remains.

[1411]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 忽見 - Sudden sight or sudden appearance; indicates an abrupt visual experience.
  • 深處 - A remote or hidden place; often used to describe a mysterious or secluded location.
  • 有籠燈 - Lanterns in cages – a poetic or symbolic expression for light sources, possibly indicating a supernatural or ceremonial setting.
  • 眾喜曰 - The crowd was delighted and said – a common narrative structure in Classical Chinese to indicate a collective reaction.
  • 龍飛相公施食矣 - Master Longfei is offering food – a term of reverence or honorific address. '龍飛' (Longfei) is a name or title, and '施食' (offering food) may refer to a spiritual or charitable act.
  • 邀戴同往 - They invited Dai to go together – a polite invitation to accompany someone.
  • 戴慮水沮 - Dai was concerned about the water being muddy – a metaphor or literal concern about obstacles in the water.
  • 眾強曳扶以行 - The crowd forcefully pulled and supported him to walk – indicates physical assistance or coercion.
  • 飄若履虛 - Floating as if walking on air – a poetic description of lightness or ethereal movement.
  • 曲折半里許 - Twisting and turning for about half a li – a description of a winding path.
  • 至一处 - Arrived at a place – a common narrative phrase.
  • 眾釋令自行 - The crowd released him, allowing him to go on his own – a shift in control from the group to the individual.
  • 步益上 - He walked upward more – a description of ascending.
  • 如升數仞之階 - Like climbing a staircase several ren high – a metaphor for ascending a great height.
  • 階盡 - When the steps ended – the end of a sequence.
  • 睹房廊 - Saw houses and corridors – a description of a building or structure.
  • 堂上燒明燭一支 - A single bright candle was burning on the hall – a description of a lit space.
  • 大如臂 - As large as an arm – a simile for size.
  • 戴久不見火光 - Dai had not seen firelight for a long time – indicates surprise or a long absence of light.
  • 喜極趨上 - With great joy, he rushed upward – an emotional reaction to seeing light.
  • 上坐一叟 - An old man was sitting above – a description of a figure in a superior position.
  • 儒服儒巾 - In scholar's robes and scholar's cap – indicating a learned or scholarly appearance.
  • 戴辍步不敢前 - Dai stopped in his steps and dared not go forward – a hesitation or fear.
  • 叟已睹見 - The old man had already seen him – indicates the old man's awareness.
  • 訝問 - Surprised and asked – a common narrative structure for a character's reaction.
  • 生人何來 - Who is this stranger and where does he come from? – a direct question.
  • 戴上，伏地自陳 - Dai got up and prostrated himself, explaining himself – a gesture of submission or explanation.
  • 因令起，賜之坐 - He then had him rise and granted him a seat – a gesture of respect or authority.
  • 自言 - He said to himself – a narrative device indicating internal monologue or self-identification.
  • 戴潛，字龍飛 - Dai Qian, styled Longfei – a proper name with a given name and a style name.
  • 向因不肖孫堂，連結匪類 - Previously, because of my unworthy grandson Tang, who associated with wicked people – a statement of cause and blame.
  • 近墓作井，使老夫不安於夜室 - Near the tomb, he dug a well, causing me to be uneasy in my night chamber – a description of a disturbance.
  • 故以海水投之 - Therefore, I cast seawater into it – a metaphorical or literal action.
  • 今其後續如何矣 - What has happened to it since then? – a question about the current state.
  • 蓋戴近宗凡五支 - It is said that among Dai's recent clan there are five branches – an explanation of family structure.
  • 堂居長 - Tang is the eldest – indicating position within the family.
  • 初，邑中大姓賂堂 - Originally, the great families of the county bribed Tang – a statement of corruption or influence.
  • 攻煤於其祖茔之側 - To dig for coal near his ancestral tomb – an act of desecration or exploitation.
  • 諸弟畏其強莫敢爭 - His younger brothers feared his strength and dared not contest – a description of power dynamics.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 龍飛相公 - A title or honorific, possibly a name or a designation of a spiritual or supernatural figure. '龍飛' may be a personal name or a symbolic title.
  • 施食 - A term often associated with Buddhist or Daoist rituals, where food is offered to spirits or hungry ghosts. This may indicate a supernatural or ritualistic context.
  • 籠燈 - Lanterns in cages – this may refer to a specific type of lantern used in religious or ritual settings, possibly with symbolic or magical significance.
  • 儒服儒巾 - Scholarly attire, indicating that the old man is a learned or respected figure, possibly a sage or ancestor.
  • 伏地自陳 - Prostration and self-explanation – a traditional gesture of submission and respect in classical Chinese society.
  • 賂堂 - Bribing Tang – reflects the corruption or power struggles among the elite in the county.
  • 攻煤於其祖茔之側 - Digging for coal near an ancestral tomb – indicates a conflict between economic development and respect for ancestors, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 諸弟畏其強莫敢爭 - Fear of power – reflects the hierarchical and often oppressive nature of traditional family structures and local governance.

[1412]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 无何 - Temporal expression meaning 'not long after' or 'soon after.'
  • 地水暴至 - Literally 'water from the earth suddenly arrived,' referring to a sudden flood.
  • 采煤人尽死井中 - Literal description of coal miners dying in a mine, emphasizing the tragedy.
  • 诸死者家群兴大讼 - 'The families of the deceased all started a large lawsuit,' indicating a collective legal dispute.
  • 堂及大姓皆以此贫 - 'The family and the prominent clans all became poor because of this,' showing the financial impact on both the family and the community.
  • 堂子孙至无立锥 - 'The descendants of the family had no place to stand,' a hyperbolic expression indicating extreme poverty.
  • 戴乃堂弟裔也 - 'Dai was a descendant of the younger brother of the family,' indicating lineage.
  • 曾闻先人传其事 - 'He once heard his ancestors tell the story,' suggesting a family tradition or oral history.
  • 因告翁 - 'So he told the old man,' referring to an elder or wise person.
  • 此等不肖，其后焉得昌 - 'Such unworthy people, how could their descendants prosper?' A moral judgment on the consequences of the family's actions.
  • 汝既来此，当勿废读 - 'Since you have come here, you should not neglect your studies,' a command to continue education.
  • 因饷以酒馔 - 'He then offered him wine and food,' indicating hospitality.
  • 皆成、洪制艺 - 'All of them were works by Cheng and Hong,' referring to literary works by the scholars Cheng and Hong.
  • 迫使研读 - 'Forced to study diligently,' indicating a strict educational environment.
  • 又命题课文 - 'He also set questions for essays,' describing a teaching method.
  • 如师教徒 - 'Just like a teacher instructs his students,' emphasizing the mentorship aspect.
  • 堂上烛常明，不剪亦不灭 - 'The candles in the hall always burned, neither trimmed nor extinguished,' suggesting a continuous and uninterrupted study environment.
  • 倦时辄眠 - 'When tired, he would sleep,' indicating a flexible and relaxed approach to study.
  • 莫辨晨夕 - 'He could not distinguish between morning and evening,' showing the intensity of his study.
  • 翁时出，则以一僮给役 - 'When the old man went out, he would send a boy to serve,' indicating the provision of help when needed.
  • 历时觉有数年之久 - 'It felt like several years had passed,' a subjective perception of time.
  • 然幸无苦 - 'But fortunately, there was no hardship,' indicating a positive experience despite the intensity.
  • 但无别书可读 - 'But there were no other books to read,' highlighting the limited resources.
  • 惟制艺百首，首四千余遍矣 - 'Only a hundred essays by Cheng and Hong, each read over four thousand times,' emphasizing the repetitive nature of the study.
  • 子孽报已满，合还人世 - 'Your karma has been fulfilled, it is time to return to the human world,' a mystical or religious concept.
  • 余冢邻煤洞，阴风刺骨 - 'My tomb is near the coal mine, the cold wind is piercing,' describing a desolate and eerie location.
  • 得志后当迁我于东原 - 'After achieving success, you should move me to the eastern plain,' a request for relocation after success.
  • 戴敬诺 - 'Dai respectfully agreed,' indicating compliance with the request.
  • 翁乃唤集群鬼，仍送至旧坐处 - 'The old man then called together the ghosts and sent him back to his old seat,' a supernatural element.
  • 群鬼罗拜再嘱 - 'The ghosts surrounded him in reverence and gave final instructions,' indicating a formal and respectful farewell.
  • 戴亦不知何计可出 - 'Dai did not know what to do,' showing a moment of confusion or uncertainty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 采煤人 - Refers to coal miners, indicating a specific profession and the dangers associated with it.
  • 诸死者家群兴大讼 - The context of a lawsuit suggests a legal system where families can sue each other, possibly due to negligence or other issues.
  • 堂及大姓 - 'The family and the prominent clans' indicates a hierarchical social structure where the family is part of a larger, influential group.
  • 堂子孙至无立锥 - 'No place to stand' is a hyperbolic expression indicating extreme poverty, reflecting the severity of the situation.
  • 戴乃堂弟裔也 - 'Descendant of the younger brother' indicates a specific lineage and family relationship.
  • 曾闻先人传其事 - 'Hearing from ancestors' suggests a strong tradition of oral history and family stories.
  • 因告翁 - 'Telling the old man' implies a wise elder who can provide guidance or solutions.
  • 此等不肖，其后焉得昌 - 'Unworthy people' and 'prosperity' reflect a moral judgment and the belief in karma or divine retribution.
  • 汝既来此，当勿废读 - 'Continue your studies' emphasizes the importance of education and personal development.
  • 因饷以酒馔 - 'Offering wine and food' is a gesture of hospitality and respect.
  • 皆成、洪制艺 - 'Works by Cheng and Hong' refers to specific scholars whose works were highly regarded and studied.
  • 迫使研读 - 'Forced to study' indicates a strict and disciplined educational environment.
  • 又命题课文 - 'Setting questions for essays' is a common teaching method in classical Chinese education.
  • 如师教徒 - 'Like a teacher instructing students' emphasizes the mentorship aspect of the relationship.
  • 堂上烛常明，不剪亦不灭 - 'Candles always burning' symbolizes a continuous and uninterrupted study environment.
  • 倦时辄眠 - 'Sleeping when tired' shows a flexible and relaxed approach to study, emphasizing the importance of rest.
  • 莫辨晨夕 - 'Not distinguishing between morning and evening' indicates the intensity and dedication to study.
  • 翁时出，则以一僮给役 - 'Sending a boy to serve' shows the provision of help when needed, reflecting a supportive environment.
  • 历时觉有数年之久 - 'Feeling like several years' is a subjective perception of time, emphasizing the intensity of the experience.
  • 然幸无苦 - 'No hardship' indicates a positive experience despite the intensity.
  • 但无别书可读 - 'No other books to read' highlights the limited resources available.
  • 惟制艺百首，首四千余遍矣 - 'Reading the same essays repeatedly' emphasizes the repetitive nature of the study, reflecting the importance of memorization and mastery.
  • 子孽报已满，合还人世 - 'Karma fulfilled' is a mystical or religious concept, suggesting a belief in the consequences of one's actions.
  • 余冢邻煤洞，阴风刺骨 - 'Near the coal mine' and 'cold wind' describe a desolate and eerie location, reflecting the supernatural elements of the story.
  • 得志后当迁我于东原 - 'Move to the eastern plain' is a request for relocation after achieving success, reflecting a desire for a better life.
  • 戴敬诺 - 'Respectfully agreed' indicates compliance with the request, showing respect and obedience.
  • 翁乃唤集群鬼，仍送至旧坐处 - 'Calling together the ghosts' and 'sending back to the old seat' are supernatural elements, reflecting the mystical nature of the story.
  • 群鬼罗拜再嘱 - 'Surrounded in reverence' and 'giving final instructions' indicate a formal and respectful farewell, emphasizing the importance of the moment.
  • 戴亦不知何计可出 - 'Not knowing what to do' shows a moment of confusion or uncertainty, reflecting the complexity of the situation.

[1413]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先是家中失戴 - Before this, the family lost Dai. The phrase '失戴' is ambiguous; it could mean 'lost Dai' or 'Dai went missing.' The context suggests the latter.
  • 搜訪既窮 - After exhaustive searching. '搜訪' means searching or investigating, and '既窮' means to the point of exhaustion or limit.
  • 系缧多人 - Many people were arrested. '系缧' is an archaic term for arresting or imprisoning someone.
  • 杳无踪迹 - No trace was found. '杳无' means completely without, and '踪迹' means trace or track.
  • 积三四年 - For three or four years. '积' means accumulation or passage of time.
  • 官离任 - The official left office. '离任' means to leave one's post.
  • 缉察亦弛 - The investigation was also relaxed. '缉察' means investigation or surveillance, and '弛' means to relax or loosen.
  • 遣嫁去 - She was sent away to be married. '遣嫁' means to send someone away to get married.
  • 复治旧井 - The well was repaired again. '复治' means to repair or renovate again.
  • 入洞见戴 - He entered the cave and saw Dai. '入洞' means to enter a cave, and '见戴' means to see Dai.
  • 抚之未死 - He touched him and found he was not dead. '抚之' means to touch or pat, and '未死' means not dead.
  • 大骇 - He was greatly startled. '大骇' means to be greatly frightened or shocked.
  • 报诸其家 - He reported it to his family. '报诸' means to report to, and '其家' means his family.
  • 始能言其底里 - He could finally speak about the details. '始能' means could finally, and '言其底里' means to speak about the details or the whole story.
  • 自戴入井 - When Dai fell into the well. '自' means when or after, and '入井' means to fall into the well.
  • 邻人殴杀其妻 - The neighbor beat and killed his wife. '殴杀' means to beat and kill.
  • 为妻翁所讼 - He was sued by his wife's father. '为...所' is a passive construction, and '讼' means to sue.
  • 驳审年余 - After a year of appeals and reviews. '驳审' means appeals and reviews.
  • 仅存皮骨而归 - He returned barely alive, with skin and bones. '仅存' means barely, and '皮骨' means skin and bones.
  • 大惧亡去 - He fled in great fear. '大惧' means great fear, and '亡去' means to flee or escape.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '日过北院为马治菊' is elliptical, and the relationship between Tao and Ma is implied rather than explicitly stated.

  • The sentence '陶姊小字黄英，雅善谈，辄过吕所，与共纫绩' is a descriptive sequence that must be read as a single, coherent narrative.
  • The sentence '自食其力不为贪，贩花为业不为俗' is a moral statement that requires careful interpretation to convey the intended philosophical meaning.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '而父以涉远，又薄女子之怀春也，笑置之' is elliptical, with '而父' (the father) as the implied subject. The father's actions and attitudes are described in a concise manner.

  • 漏数万之税非私 - Literally 'leaking tens of thousands of taxes is not private,' suggesting a large-scale issue that is not individual but systemic.
  • 负升斗之盐则私之 - Literally 'carrying a handful of salt is considered private,' implying a small-scale act that is deemed personal or individual.
  • 本境售诸他境非私 - Literally 'selling within the same region to another region is not private,' indicating a transaction within the same jurisdiction.
  • 本境买诸本境则私之 - Literally 'buying within the same region from another region is considered private,' suggesting a transaction that is deemed personal or individual.
  • 律中‘盐法’最严 - Literally 'the law regarding salt is the strictest,' indicating the severity of the laws concerning salt.
  • 背负易食者不之禁 - Literally 'those who carry and exchange food are not prohibited,' suggesting that certain activities are not restricted.
  • 一切不禁 - Literally 'not prohibiting anything,' indicating a lack of restrictions.
  • 专杀此贫难军民 - Literally 'specifically killing these poor and difficult soldiers and civilians,' suggesting a targeted action against a specific group.
  • 非天下之良民乎哉 - Literally 'are they not the good people of the world?' expressing a rhetorical question about the moral standing of a group.

  • 上守法而不盗 - Literally 'those above adhere to the law and do not steal,' indicating a group that follows the law.
  • 下知耻而不倡 - Literally 'those below know shame and do not initiate,' suggesting a group that understands the importance of propriety.
  • 不得已，而揭十母而求一子 - Literally 'out of necessity, carrying ten mothers to find one son,' a metaphor for the desperate actions of the poor.
  • 夜不闭户 - Literally 'doors not closed at night,' a metaphor for a society where people do not lock their doors, indicating a high level of trust and safety.
  • 日变日诡 - Literally 'changing day by day and becoming more deceptive,' suggesting a worsening situation.
  • 顽民日生，而良民日死 - Literally 'the wicked people are increasing, while the good people are dying,' indicating a moral decline.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 盐法 - The laws and regulations concerning salt, which were often very strict in ancient China due to the importance of salt as a commodity and its role in the economy.
  • 三尺法 - A metaphor for the law, derived from the height of a legal text or the length of a bamboo slip used for writing laws.
  • 淘河 - A metaphorical expression for enduring hardship, often used to describe the suffering of common people.
  • 背负易食者 - Refers to people who carry and exchange food, often in the context of small-scale trade or barter.
  • 贫难军民 - Refers to poor and difficult soldiers and civilians, highlighting the plight of the underprivileged.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening sequence uses a series of contrasts to highlight the injustice of the situation.
  • This clause contrasts the strict laws with the lack of restrictions and the resulting harm to the poor.
  • This clause describes the moral and ethical behavior of the poor and the potential for a harmonious society if they were treated fairly.
  • This clause uses a metaphor to criticize the merchants for their exploitative practices.
  • This clause criticizes the officials for accepting bribes and using the law to harm the good people.
  • This clause suggests that the poor might turn to more extreme measures due to the lack of fair treatment.
  • This concluding clause summarizes the moral decay and the worsening conditions of the poor.

  • 无赖子 - 指的是不良子弟或流氓。
  • 抛粟毁器，盎盂无存 - 表示对财物的破坏，其中‘盎盂’是古代用来盛饭的器皿。
  • 周告官 - 古代民间纠纷可以通过向官府告状来解决。
  • 官怒，拘孝等囚系之，将行申黜 - 古代官府有权逮捕和惩罚违法者。
  • 友于惧，见宰自投 - 古代犯错的人可以通过自首来减轻罪责。
  • 友于品行，素为宰重 - 古代官员重视品德高尚的人。
  • 诸兄弟以是得无苦 - 古代家族内部的纠纷有时可以通过内部调解解决。
  • 友于乃诣周所负荆 - 古代表示请罪的行为，背负荆条是古代的一种请罪方式。
  • 周亦器重友于，讼遂止 - 古代民间纠纷可以通过双方的和解来解决。
  • 孝归，终不德友于 - 古代家族内部的矛盾有时会持续很长时间。
  • 无何，友于母张夫人卒 - 古代丧葬礼仪严格，不按照礼制服丧会被视为不孝。
  • 孝等不为服，宴饮如故 - 古代丧葬礼仪中，丧期和丧服有严格的规定。
  • 仁、义益忿 - 古代家族内部的矛盾可能导致兄弟之间的冲突。
  • 及葬，把持墓门，不使合厝 - 古代丧葬礼仪中，合葬是一种常见的做法。
  • 友于乃瘗母隧道中 - 古代丧葬礼仪中，隧道葬是一种特殊的葬法。
  • 未几孝妻亡，友于招仁、义同往奔丧 - 古代丧葬礼仪中，奔丧是一种重要的礼仪。
  • 再劝之，哄然散去 - 古代人们在面对冲突时，有时会选择逃避。
  • 友于乃自往，临哭尽哀 - 古代丧葬礼仪中，亲自前往哭泣是一种表达哀悼的方式。
  • 隔墙闻仁、义鼓且吹 - 古代丧葬礼仪中，吹鼓乐是一种常见的仪式。
  • 孝怒，纠诸弟往殴之 - 古代家族内部的矛盾可能导致暴力冲突。
  • 友于操杖先从 - 古代人们在面对冲突时，有时会选择自卫。
  • 入其家，仁觉先逃 - 古代人们在面对冲突时，有时会选择逃避。
  • 兴方逾垣，友于自后击仆之 - 古代人们在面对冲突时，有时会选择自卫。
  • 孝等拳杖交加，殴不止 - 古代家族内部的矛盾可能导致暴力冲突。
  • 友于横身障阻之 - 古代人们在面对冲突时，有时会选择自卫。
  • 孝怒，让友于 - 古代人们在面对冲突时，有时会选择责备对方。

Proper Nouns

  • 张夫人 - 友于的母亲。

[1606]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 我不怙弟恶，亦不助兄暴 - A moral statement expressing the speaker’s refusal to condone either the wrongdoing of their younger brother or the violence of their older brother. '怙' means to protect or support, and '暴' refers to violent or unjust behavior.
  • 如怒不解，愿以身代之 - A conditional statement meaning 'If anger cannot be resolved, I am willing to take his place (in punishment).' This is a strong expression of personal sacrifice.
  • 孝遂反杖挞友于 - '孝' turned around and beat '友于' with a stick.
  • 忠、信亦相助殴兄 - '忠' and '信' joined in beating the older brother.
  • 声震里党 - The commotion was loud enough to be heard throughout the village.
  • 群集劝解，乃散去 - A crowd gathered to try to calm things down, and then they dispersed.
  • 友于即扶杖诣兄请罪 - '友于' went to his brother to ask for forgiveness, likely supporting on a staff due to injury.
  • 孝逐去之，不令居丧次 - '孝' sent him away and did not allow him to stay in the mourning house.
  • 而义创甚，不复食饮 - '义' was seriously injured and no longer ate or drank.
  • 仁代具词讼官 - '仁' prepared a legal statement to take to the authorities.
  • 诉其不为庶母行服 - Accused of not performing the mourning rites for his stepmother.
  • 官签拘孝、忠、信，而令友于陈状 - Officials ordered the detention of '孝、忠、信' and instructed '友于' to submit his own statement.
  • 友于以面目损伤，不能诣署 - '友于' could not go to the office due to facial injury from the beating.
  • 但作词禀白，哀求寝息 - Wrote a statement to report and pleaded for the case to be dropped.
  • 宰遂消案 - The local magistrate closed the case.
  • 义亦寻愈 - '义' soon recovered.
  • 由是仇怨益深 - The enmity became even deeper.
  • 仁、义皆幼弱，辄被敲楚 - '仁' and '义' were young and weak, and thus vulnerable to physical punishment.
  • 人皆有兄弟，我独无 - A lamentation meaning 'Everyone has brothers, but I am alone without one.'
  • 此两语，我宜言之，两弟何云 - The speaker says that he himself should be the one to say 'I have no brothers,' not his younger brothers.
  • 因苦劝之，卒不听 - The speaker strongly urged '友于' but was finally not listened to.
  • 遂扃户，携妻子借寓他所，离家五十余里，冀不相闻 - '友于' locked the door, took his wife and children, rented a place elsewhere, and hoped they would not hear from each other.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 行服 - A Confucian mourning practice in which one wears specific clothing and refrains from certain activities to express grief over the death of a relative.
  • 庶母 - A stepmother, or a mother of one's father’s concubine. In Confucian society, mourning for a stepmother is considered a duty, though not as strict as mourning for a biological mother.
  • 里党 - Refers to the local community or neighborhood. In classical Chinese society, the 'li' was a unit of administrative and social organization.
  • 讼官 - Refers to the legal process of bringing a case before a local magistrate or official.
  • 宰 - A term for a local magistrate or county official.
  • 消案 - A legal term meaning 'to close the case' or 'to dismiss the case.'
  • 敲楚 - A term referring to physical punishment, such as beating or flogging.
  • 五十余里 - A distance of more than fifty li, a classical Chinese unit of distance. One li is approximately 500 meters, so this is over 25 kilometers.

Proper Nouns

  • 孝 - A name, likely the elder brother or a family member who acted against '友于.'
  • 忠 - A name, likely one of the brothers who supported '孝.'
  • 信 - Another name, likely the third brother who supported '孝.'
  • 友于 - A name, likely the younger brother who was the victim of the beating and later the subject of the legal dispute.
  • 仁 - A name, likely another younger brother who prepared a legal statement.
  • 义 - A name, likely another family member who was seriously injured.

[1607]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 友于在家雖不助弟，而孝等尚稍有顧忌 - Although the eldest brother does not help his younger brothers at home, his filial piety and other virtues still make him somewhat cautious.
  • 既去，諸兄一不当，辄叫骂其门，辱侵母讳 - Once he left, whenever the other brothers acted improperly, they would shout and curse at his door, defaming and insulting their mother’s name.
  • 仁、义度不能抗，惟杜门思乘间刺杀之，行则怀刀 - Ren and Yi were unable to resist, so they closed their doors and thought of killing him when the opportunity arose, carrying a knife with them.
  • 忽携妇亡归 - suddenly brought his wife back home.
  • 诸兄弟以家久析，聚谋三日，竟无处可以置之 - The other brothers, because the family had been separated for a long time, gathered together for three days, but still could not find a place to accommodate him.
  • 友于喜，归，共出田宅居成 - The eldest brother was happy, returned home, and together they provided land and houses for Cheng.
  • 诸兄怒其市惠，登门窘辱 - The other brothers were angry that he was showing off his kindness, and they came to his door to humiliate him.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 友于 - Refers to the eldest brother, often seen as a figure of authority and responsibility in traditional Chinese family structures.
  • 孝等 - Refers to filial piety and other virtues, which are highly valued in Confucian ethics.
  • 母讳 - Refers to the mother’s name, which is considered sacred and should not be defamed or insulted.
  • 市惠 - Means 'showing off kindness' or 'being overly generous,' which can be seen as insincere or manipulative in certain contexts.

Proper Nouns

  • 友于 - The eldest brother.
  • 仁、义 - Two individuals, likely younger brothers.
  • 成 - The returned brother.
  • 诸兄 - The other brothers.
